name: M142 HIMARS (High Mobility Artillery Rocket System)
detail: US-supplied; first delivered June 2022. HIMARS with GMLRS rockets (range 70km) transformed the battlefield by allowing Ukraine to strike Russian ammunition depots, command posts, and logistics far behind the front line. Ukraine received 38 launchers by 2024. Russia unable to consistently intercept; forced tactical changes. Credited with neutralizing Russian artillery advantage.

name: FGM-148 Javelin ATGM
detail: US-supplied; over 8,500 launchers and 30,000+ missiles provided. Fire-and-forget top-attack capability devastates Russian armor. Symbolized Ukrainian resistance in early weeks of the invasion. Combined with NLAWs (UK) to destroy Russia's 40-mile convoy north of Kyiv. Unit cost ~$240,000; destroyed Russian tanks worth $3-7M each.

name: Patriot PAC-3 Air Defense System
detail: US and German-supplied; first battery deployed spring 2023. Patriot systems provide Ukraine's highest-tier air defense against ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, and aircraft. Ukraine has operationally shot down multiple Russian aircraft including Su-35s with Patriot. US approved additional batteries in 2024 after Russian Kharkiv offensive. Finite interceptor supply is a critical constraint.

name: Leopard 2 (A4/A6 variants)
detail: German, Polish, Finnish, Norwegian, Spanish-supplied; approximately 100 delivered 2023-24. Germany's authorization of Leopard 2 supply in January 2023 — after months of pressure — unlocked deliveries from multiple European nations. Deployed in the 2023 counteroffensive; some were lost to Russian mines and ATGMs. More modern and maintainable than Soviet-era T-72s.

name: F-16 Fighting Falcon
detail: Netherlands, Denmark, Belgium, and Norway committed to supplying 85+ F-16s. First aircraft arrived in Ukraine mid-2024. Ukraine receives F-16 Block 20/25 variants capable of firing AIM-120 AMRAAM and AIM-9X. Primary roles: air defense, electronic warfare, standoff strike. Numbers too small for air superiority; one lost in August 2024. Ukrainian pilots trained in EU countries.

name: MGM-140 ATACMS (Army Tactical Missile System)
detail: US-supplied secretly from October 2023 (cluster warhead version), then authorized for deep strikes inside Russia in November 2024. Range: 300km. Ukraine used to strike Berdyansk and Luhansk airfields in initial secret deliveries, destroying multiple Russian helicopters. Later authorized strikes on Bryansk Oblast military targets. Russia updated nuclear doctrine in response.

name: Storm Shadow / SCALP-EG Cruise Missiles
detail: UK (Storm Shadow) and France (SCALP-EG) supplied; deliveries began May 2023. Range: 250km. Stealth cruise missiles capable of penetrating hardened targets — used by Ukraine to strike Russian HQ, ammunition depots, and the Kerch Bridge approach. UK initially restricted to strikes within Ukraine; restriction later lifted for some targets in Russia.

name: Bayraktar TB2 Unmanned Combat Aerial Vehicle
detail: Turkish-supplied; Ukraine had 50+ TB2 drones before the invasion. TB2 proved devastating against Russian armor and logistics in early war (March-April 2022), until Russian EW and air defense adapted. Remains operationally active for reconnaissance and strike in areas with degraded Russian air defense. Symbol of Ukrainian drone warfare capability.

name: Shahed-136/Geran-2 Loitering Munition
detail: Iran-supplied; Russia received hundreds then began domestic production as 'Geran-2.' Russia uses them in mass waves — up to 100 per night — targeting Ukrainian power infrastructure, particularly in autumn/winter campaigns. Ukraine intercepts 60-80% with air defense. Cost-effective for Russia (~$50,000/unit vs. $1M+ for Patriot interceptors).

name: Kalibr (SS-N-27) Naval Cruise Missile
detail: Russian-manufactured; fired from Black Sea Fleet ships and submarines. Range: 1,500-2,500km. Used extensively to strike Ukrainian cities and infrastructure. Ukraine shot down 80%+ with air defense by 2023. Production reportedly stressed by Western sanctions on microelectronics. Also fired from Caspian Sea vessels early in conflict.

name: M2 Bradley Infantry Fighting Vehicle
detail: US-supplied; approximately 300 delivered. The Bradley's 25mm autocannon penetrates Russian BMP infantry fighting vehicles. Deployed in the 2023 counteroffensive and credited with high Russian armor kill ratios. Some lost to mines. Combined with Leopard 2 for combined arms maneuver that proved difficult against Russian minefields lacking close air support.

name: R-360 Neptune Anti-Ship Missile
detail: Ukrainian-manufactured; became globally famous when it sank the Russian missile cruiser Moskva on April 13, 2022 — the largest warship sunk in combat since the Falklands War. The loss of the Black Sea Fleet flagship forced Russia to withdraw its ships from the northwestern Black Sea, enabling Ukraine to defend Odesa and eventually resume grain exports.
